33.reason: [Error: i is incremented before reading p[i]->n, so p[i] is not yet allocated. Also, the loop condition uses EOF but input ends with newline, not EOF, causing infinite loop or incorrect reading.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Error: i is incremented before reading p[i]->n, so p[i] is not yet allocated. Also, the loop condition uses EOF but input ends with newline, not EOF, causing infinite loop or incorrect reading.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 0.25 , reason:  Error: i is incremented before reading p[i]->n, causing out-of-bounds access and incorrect reading
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Error: p[i] accessed before allocation; p[i] for exponent is after increment but not yet allocated, writing to invalid memory]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 0.14285714285714285 , reason:  Undefined behavior: uses p[i] after increment for second argument without proper allocation; also p[i] may not be allocated for i>0.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Error: Logic error in reading input. p[i++] increments i, so &p[i]->n accesses the next uninitialized pointer. Also, scanf format with trailing space may cause hanging or incorrect parsing depending on input. The loop structure is fundamentally flawed for reading two polynomials sequentially.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  [Error: Logic error in reading input. The code attempts to read into p[i] (which is uninitialized or NULL if i increments before allocation) and then allocates for the next index. It also assumes all input belongs to one polynomial initially, failing to distinguish between the two polynomials as required by the problem description. The input format requires reading two separate polynomials.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.2 , reason:  [Indexing error: after i++, p[i] is uninitialized when accessing p[i]->n. Also, the loop condition uses a trailing space in format string which may cause scanf to wait for more input.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.2 , reason:  // Error: Incrementing i before using it to access p[i]->n leads to undefined behavior. Also, p[i] is not allocated before use.
